coredata.dc:title: Highly selective and sensitive ratiometric detection of Hg2+ ions with NFS co-doped carbon dots: Real sample analysis, antibacterial properties, and cellular imaging applications

coredata.dc:description: A simple, low-cost hydrothermal method was employed to synthesize highly fluorescent nitrogen-, fluorine-, and sulfur-co-doped carbon dots (NFS-CDs) using flufenamic acid and L-cysteine as precursors. The synthesized NFS-CDs exhibited dual emission peaks at 490 and 580 nm with a quantum yield of 24.7 %. They exhibit excellent stability, excitation-dependent fluorescent, and particle sizes ranging from 2 to 8 nm. The fluorescent chemosensor probe, NFS-CDs, showed strong selectivity and sensitivity for Hg2+ over other metal ions investigated in aqueous solutions (pH ∼ 7.4). Strong fluorescent enhancement at 490 nm and considerable quenching at 580 nm was observed in the presence of Hg2+ ions. The stoichiometric ratio of the NFS-CDs/Hg2+ complex was optimized to 1:1 according to the Benesi-Hildebrand and Stern-Volmer plot methods. The NFS-CDs exhibited a linear dynamic detection range from 0 to 10 × 10−6 M for Hg2+ ions with a lower detection limit of 18.0 and 67.5 × 10−9 M, respectively, at 490 and 580 nm. Practical applications of NFS-CDs in detecting Hg2+ ions in natural water samples showed high recovery rates (98.9–104.6 %) and low relative standard deviation (RSD ≤ 2.47 %). The NFS-CDs/Hg2+ achieved 78.7 ± 2.6 % and 83.4 ± 2.3 % antibacterial activity against E. coli and S. aureus as NFS-CDs/Hg2+ could damage the bacterial walls when they entered the bacteria. Furthermore, the NFS-CDs were used to detect Hg2+ ions intracellularly in HCT116 cells with low toxicity using live cell imaging.

(b) FL excitation and emission spectrum of the NFS-CDs. (c) FL spectra of the NFS-CDs at various excitation wavelengths from 340 to 440 nm.
Fluorescent spectral intensity of the NFS-CDs at different pH values (a), 0–1.0 M NaCl ionic strength (b), different time interval (c), and storage stability with the excitation wavelength of 390 nm.
(a) FTIR spectra, (b) XRDN pattern, (c) TEM analysis, and (d) particle size measurements of the NFS-CDs.
(a) XPS survey scan spectra of NFS-CDs and High-resolution XPS spectra of (b) N1s, (c) F1s, (d) S2p, (e) C1s, and (f) O1s.
(a) Fluorescent spectra, (b) Intensity profile, and (c) daylight and UV light photographic images of NFS-CDs in the presence of various metal ions (10 × 10−6 M) in pH ∼ 7.4 solutions.

(a) Fluorescent spectra of NFS-CDs in the presence of Hg2+ ions (0–10 × 10−6 M) in pH ∼ 7.4 solutions. (b) Emission intensity profile of NFS-CDs in the presence of Hg2+ ions (0–10 × 10−6 M) in pH ∼ 7.4 solutions at 490 and 580 nm.

head.ce:title: Highly selective and sensitive ratiometric detection of Hg2+ ions with NFS co-doped carbon dots: Real sample analysis, antibacterial properties, and cellular imaging applications

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A simple, low-cost hydrothermal method was employed to synthesize highly fluorescent nitrogen-, fluorine-, and sulfur-co-doped carbon dots (NFS-CDs) using flufenamic acid and L-cysteine as precursors. The synthesized NFS-CDs exhibited dual emission peaks at 490 and 580 nm with a quantum yield of 24.7 %. They exhibit excellent stability, excitation-dependent fluorescent, and particle sizes ranging from 2 to 8 nm. The fluorescent chemosensor probe, NFS-CDs, showed strong selectivity and sensitivity for Hg2+ over other metal ions investigated in aqueous solutions (pH ∼ 7.4). Strong fluorescent enhancement at 490 nm and considerable quenching at 580 nm was observed in the presence of Hg2+ ions. The stoichiometric ratio of the NFS-CDs/Hg2+ complex was optimized to 1:1 according to the Benesi-Hildebrand and Stern-Volmer plot methods. The NFS-CDs exhibited a linear dynamic detection range from 0 to 10 × 10−6 M for Hg2+ ions with a lower detection limit of 18.0 and 67.5 × 10−9 M, respectively, at 490 and 580 nm. Practical applications of NFS-CDs in detecting Hg2+ ions in natural water samples showed high recovery rates (98.9–104.6 %) and low relative standard deviation (RSD ≤ 2.47 %). The NFS-CDs/Hg2+ achieved 78.7 ± 2.6 % and 83.4 ± 2.3 % antibacterial activity against E. coli and S. aureus as NFS-CDs/Hg2+ could damage the bacterial walls when they entered the bacteria. Furthermore, the NFS-CDs were used to detect Hg2+ ions intracellularly in HCT116 cells with low toxicity using live cell imaging.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Mercury (Hg2+) is highly toxic to humans and wildlife, posing severe risks to the nervous system, kidneys, and other organs [1]. Chronic exposure to Hg2+can lead to poisoning with profound health implications. Hg2+ ions in water can easily accumulate in the human body through the food chain, posing significant health risks even at very low concentrations [2,3]. It can be transformed into various chemical forms, including organic mercury compounds, through microbial activity, which increases its mobility and toxicity. Hg2+ ions have been used in various industrial applications, including the production of chlorine and caustic soda (chlor-alkali process), electrical switches, and certain types of batteries. However, owing to their toxicity, many of these applications have been phased out or regulated. Stringent regulations are in place for the use, disposal, and environmental release of Hg and its compounds, aimed at reducing human and environmental exposure [4]. Therefore, detecting even trace amounts of Hg2+ in water with high selectivity and sensitivity is crucial. Currently, techniques such as inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [5], atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS) [6], and cold vapor atomic fluorescence spectroscopy (CVAFS) [7] are used to identify and quantify Hg2+. However, these methods often involve complex procedures and expensive equipment, limiting their practicality for routine mercury monitoring. The development of a rapid, easy, and affordable technique for sensing trace Hg2+ in water remains challenging. Fluorescent sensors have emerged as key tools for the direct and instant monitoring of Hg2+ ions [8–10] in biological samples, offering advantages such as cost-effectiveness, ease of use, and practical applications in fieldwork compared to traditional analytical methods. Additionally, nanoparticle-based fluorescent (FL) assays for Hg2+ detection [11] have been developed due to their high sensitivity, rapid analysis, and non-destructive nature. Gold nanoparticles [12], silver nanoparticles [13], CdTe quantum dots [14], and carbon dots (CDs) [15] have been reported as effective probes for Hg2+ detection using FL techniques.

Carbon dots (CDs) have gained considerable attention in nanotechnology owing to their distinctive characteristics, such as excellent biocompatibility, stability, and adjustable fluorescent [16]. CDs, which are typically smaller than 10 nm, display exceptional optical characteristics that make them suitable for various applications, including bioimaging, drug delivery, photocatalysis, and optoelectronic devices [17]. Recently, there has been a growing interest in heteroatom-doped CDs, designed to enhance the fluorescence properties of CDs and broaden their use in live cell and sensor applications [18]. Doping CDs with heteroatoms such as nitrogen (N), sulfur (S), fluorine (F), phosphorus (P), and boron (B) can introduce new energy levels within the CDs bandgap [19]. This modification alters the electronic structure and increases the fluorescence quantum yield, allowing for tunable emission wavelengths. Additionally, the heteroatoms on the surface of CDs can create various functional groups that enhance their chemical reactivity and interaction with other molecules, making them more effective for sensing and catalysis applications [20]. In many cases, CDs doped with multiple heteroatoms exhibit stronger fluorescence than those doped with single heteroatoms. Consequently, significant progress has been made in the development of various heteroatom CD-based fluorescent techniques for detecting metal ions such as Pb2+ [21], Hg2+ [22], Cd2+ [23], Cr3+ [24], and Ag+ [25]. In either case, the fluorescence is quenched (turned off) or enhanced (turned on) by chelating metal ions with the CD functional groups.

In quantitative fluorescence analysis, two frequently used strategies are turn-off and turn-on fluorescence methods. These approaches involve measuring either the absolute intensity of a single emission peak or the intensity ratio between two distinct emission peaks. The ratiometric approach, which measures the intensity ratio of two peaks, offers more accurate and sensitive measurements. This is due to the inherent correction for variations in the light source, instrument sensitivity, and background matrix. Several dual-emission ratiometric sensors have been developed to detect ions, such as Cu2+ [26], Hg2+ [27], Fe3+ [28], and Cd2+ [29]. Despite these strides, the development of ratiometric nanosensors utilizing CDs for the precise and selective detection of heavy-metal ions is still demand. One promising approach for Hg2+ detection is the use of heteroatom-doped CDs, which have gained attention because of their unique fluorescence characteristics. Typically, the presence of Hg2+ influences the fluorescence of these doped CDs, either by quenching or enhancing their emission. Understanding the interaction mechanism between Hg2+ and CDs is crucial for developing sensitive and selective detection methods. When Hg2+ interacts with the functional groups on the surface of the doped CDs, it can lead to non-radiative recombination of excitons, resulting in fluorescence quenching [30]. This quenching occurs because the binding of Hg2+ provides a pathway for non-radiative decay, thereby diminishing the fluorescence intensity. Conversely, in some instances, Hg2+ binding can passivate non-radiative recombination sites on the CDs. This passivation leads to an increase in fluorescence intensity as the non-radiative decay pathways are inhibited. One of the notable advantages of heteroatom-doped CDs for Hg2+ detection is their short response time. Fluorescence changes occurred almost immediately upon interaction with Hg2+, allowing for quick and efficient detection. This rapid response is particularly beneficial for environmental monitoring and clinical diagnostics, where timely detection is critical.

Herein, we present a simple and cost-effective hydrothermal method for the synthesis of highly fluorescent nitrogen-, fluorine-, and sulfur-doped carbon dots (NFS-CDs) using flufenamic acid and l-cysteine and their application in Hg2+ ions sensing and biomedical applications (Scheme 1 ). These NFS-CDs exhibited excellent fluorescence, stability, and excitation-dependent fluorescent properties. They exhibited dual emission peaks at 490 and 580 nm, particle sizes between 2–8 nm, and quantum yields of 24.7 %. The NFS-CDs acted as fluorescent chemosensors with high selectivity and sensitivity for Hg2+ ions in aqueous solutions (pH ∼ 7.4), demonstrating significant fluorescent changes upon Hg2+ ion addition. The stoichiometric ratio of the NFS-CDs/Hg2+ complex was 1:1. The detection ranged from 0 to 10 × 10−6 M, with limits of 18.0 and 67.5 × 10−9 M at 490 and 580 nm, respectively. Additionally, the NFS-CDs/Hg2+ complex exhibited up to 83.4 % antibacterial activity against E. coli and S. aureus. The NFS-CDs also proved effective in live cell imaging for intracellular Hg2+ detection in HCT116 cells with low cytotoxicity.

Flufenamic acid (FFA) and L-Cysteine (CYS) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich (Seoul, South Korea). All metal salts were used in this study, and their nitrate (Co(NO3)2, Mg(NO3)2, and Pb(NO3)2) and chloride salts (AgCl, CdCl2, CeCl3, CrCl3, CuCl2, FeCl3, HgCl2, NiCl2, SnCl2, and ZnCl2) were obtained from TCI Chemicals and Sigma Aldrich, South Korea. All chemicals were of analytical grade and were used without additional purification. Ultrapure water sourced from a Milli-Q ultrapure system (18.2 MΩ cm) was employed in all experimental procedures.

Briefly, 1.0 g of FFA and 0.5 g of CYS were dissolved into 25 mL deionized water separate solutions and until fully dissolved for homogeneous solutions. Transfer the mixture solution into a clean, properly sealed Teflon-equipped stainless steel autoclave to avoid leakage during the hydrothermal reaction in an oven at 180 °C for 12 h. After the reaction, the solution was cooled to room temperature, carefully extracted, and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 30 min. Then, the supernatant containing NFS-CDs was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane to eliminate large particles. The pure filtrate solution was put in a clean container and dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C for 24 h to obtain solid carbon dots, storing them in a sealed container at 4 °C to maintain stability and prevent degradation.

Hg2+ ions were detected at RT in an aqueous solution of NFS-CDs at pH 7.4, using PBS buffer (10 mM) and excitation at 390 nm. In a typical assay, 10 μL of NFS-CDs dispersion (10 mg/mL) was diluted to 2.0 mL with the PBS buffer solution. The selectivity of NFS-CDs was tested against various interfering metal ions, including AgCl (Ag+), CdCl2 (Cd2+), CeCl3 (Ce3+), CrCl3 (Cr3+), Co(NO3)2 (Co2+), CuCl2 (Cu2+), FeCl3 (Fe3+), HgCl2 (Hg2+), Mg(NO3)2 (Mg2+), NiCl2 (Ni2+), Pb(NO3)2 (Pb2+), SnCl2 (Sn2+), and ZnCl2 (Zn2+) (10 × 10−6 M) under identical conditions. The fluorescent intensities of the NFS-CDs in the absence (F0) and presence (F) of these interfering ions were measured. Various concentrations of Hg2+ were added and mixed thoroughly prior to characterization. For the interference tests, the NFS-CDs solutions were mixed with each interfering ion (10 × 10−6 M), followed by the addition of Hg2+ ions at the same concentration (10 × 10−6 M). The prepared suspension was allowed to react for 2 min at room temperature, after which fluorescence spectra were recorded.

To evaluate the antibacterial activity of NFS-CDs and NFS-CDs/Hg2+, bacterial cultures of Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus were prepared by overnight cultivation in Luria-Bertani (LB) medium at 37 °C with constant shaking until the mid-logarithmic phase. The optical density (OD) of the bacterial cultures was measured at 600 nm, and the bacterial suspensions were adjusted to a predetermined concentration of 1 × 106 colony-forming units/mL (CFU)/mL. Subsequently, a series of wells were prepared in a 96-well plate, each containing LB medium supplemented with various concentrations of NFS-CDs or NFS-CDs/Hg2+. These wells were inoculated with the adjusted bacterial suspensions and incubated at 37 °C for 24 h with constant shaking. Following incubation, the OD at 600 nm was measured to assess bacterial growth inhibition, and the surviving bacteria were quantified using the colony counting method by plating appropriate dilutions on LB agar plates and counting the colonies after overnight incubation.

Cytotoxicity of the synthesized NFS-CDs was evaluated in HCT116 cells using a water-soluble tetrazolium (WST) salt assay. HCT116 cells were seeded in 96-well plates at a density of 2 × 105 cells/mL and allowed to adhere for 12 h. Each well is shaken for 10 min to ensure uniform cell distribution, followed by the addition of 150 μL of DMSO and further incubation for 4 h at 37 °C. After stabilization, NFS-CD solutions ranging from 0 to 150 μg/mL are added to the cells, which are then incubated without DMEM for 24 h. After incubation, the absorbance of each well was measured at 490 nm using a microplate reader (Bio-Rad) to assess cell viability. The absorbance values were analyzed to determine the cytotoxic effects of the NFS-CDs on HCT116 cells, and statistical analysis was performed to evaluate the significance of the observed effects.

Cellular imaging of HCT116 cells was used to assess the potential application of the NFS-CDs and NFS-CDs/Hg2+ solutions for biolabeling. HCT-116 cells are initially cultured in DMEM supplemented with 10 % FBS and incubated for 24 h to ensure proper cell adhesion and growth. Subsequently, the cells were treated with NFS-CDs/Hg2+ solution after eliminating the supernatant and washing with PBS. Incubation with NFS-CDs (100 μg/mL) in combination with Hg2+ ions was performed for 2 h to allow cellular uptake and interaction. Subsequently, live cell imaging was conducted using confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM; Olympus, Japan, IX-81) to visualize the fluorescent signals emitted by NFS-CDs/Hg2+ within the cellular environment. The acquired images were then analyzed to assess the intracellular distribution and fluorescent intensity, providing insights into the interface between the NFS-CDs and Hg2+ ions in HCT116 cells.

The optimal NFS-CDs were characterized using UV–Vis and FL emission spectrophotometry to examine their optical properties. The absorption, excitation, and emission spectra of the NFS-CD solutions are shown in Fig. 1 . The UV–Vis spectrum of NFS-CD displayed an absorption peak centered at 276 nm, ascribed to the π–π* transition of the aromatic sp2 domains (Fig. 1a) [31]. In addition, the absorption peaks observed at 378 nm were probably attributed to the n–π* transition of the CO groups present on the surface of the NFS-CDs [32]. The NFS-CDs were well-dispersed, forming a transparent solution under daylight and exhibiting intense green fluorescent under UV irradiation (inset of Fig. 1a). The high excitation and emission peaks of the NFS-CDs were observed at 390 and 490/580 nm, respectively (Fig. 1b). A longer emission wavelength indicates significant potential for deep tissue penetration in bio-related applications. Fig. 1c shows the FL emissions of the NFS-CD solution at various excitation wavelengths. With the excitation wavelength ranging from 340 to 440 nm, the FL emission peak showed minimal variation, consistently hovering at approximately 490 and 580 nm. The consistent stability indicates that the incorporation of N, F, and S atoms regulates both the size and surface state of the carbon dots, leading to a uniform size distribution and surface state across the NFS-CDs [33]. The quantum yield of the NFS-CDs was 24.7 %, reflecting their strong FL behavior. This is further supported by the green emission observed under 365 nm UV lamp irradiation (inset of Fig. 1a), which is attributed to the self-passivation of nitrogen, fluorine, sulfur, and oxygen functional groups on the NFS-CDs.

In practical sensing applications, the essential requirements of CDs include excellent water solubility and fluorescence stability of carbon dots. To verify the stability of the NFS-CDs, we evaluated the effects of pH, ionic strength, prolonged illumination, and storage time on FL intensity. The FL intensity was tested over a pH range of 3–11. Between pH 3 and 6, the FL intensity of the NFS-CDs increased with increasing pH; however, it decreased as the pH increased from 8 to 11 (Fig. 2 a). The highest FL intensity was observed at pH 7, indicating that the FL intensity of the NFS-CDs is closely related to pH [34]. Therefore, subsequent metal ion detection was performed in PBS solution (pH ∼ 7.4). Further, FL studies showed that the NFS-CDs remained stable and highly fluorescent, even in the presence of high salt (NaCl) concentrations (Fig. 2b), making them suitable for diverse applications, especially in the presence of high ionic strengths [35]. Fig. 2c shows that the FL intensities of the NFS-CDs remained unchanged after 30 min of UV irradiation, indicating an excellent photobleaching resistance. Moreover, as illustrated in Fig. 2d, the FL properties of the NFS-CDs remained stable even after three months of storage in air at room temperature [34]. These findings highlight the potential of the NFS-CDs for sensing applications under both physiological and environmental conditions.

The synthesized NFS-CDs were subjected to a comprehensive characterization process using a variety of surface and morphological analysis techniques, including Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FT-IR), X-ray Diffraction (XRD), Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), and High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HRTEM). These methods were employed to gain deeper insight into the chemical composition, surface functional groups, and structural morphology of the NFS-CDs. FTIR spectroscopy (Fig. 3 a) was used to examine the chemical structure and identify surface functional groups present in the NFS-CDs. Several prominent absorption peaks were observed, which provided evidence for the presence of specific chemical bonds. The absorption bands located at 3251 cm−1 and 3100 cm−1 were assigned to the characteristic vibrational frequencies of hydroxyl (OH) and amine (NH) groups. These functional groups are likely present on the surface of the NFS-CDs due to the precursors used in the synthesis process, confirming their incorporation into the carbon dot structure [36]. These groups are known to contribute to the hydrophilic nature of the particles and may play a significant role in enhancing the solubility and dispersion of the NFS-CDs in aqueous environments. Broad absorption peaks at 2878 cm−1 and 2670 cm−1 were attributed to the bending and stretching vibrations of the CS and SH bonds, respectively [37]. The presence of these sulfur-containing bonds is indicative of the successful doping of sulfur atoms onto the surface of the carbon dots, an essential feature for modulating their optical and electronic properties. In addition to sulfur-based functional groups, the peaks detected at 1452 cm−1 and 1382 cm−1 correspond to the vibrational modes of CC and CN bonds, suggesting the co-doping of nitrogen in the NFS-CDs. These bonds reflect the graphitic and amorphous carbon framework within the structure, which may contribute to enhanced conductivity and potential catalytic activity of the carbon dots.

Significantly, an intense peak at 1667 cm−1, corresponding to the CO (carbonyl) stretching vibration, was observed. This strong vibration indicates the presence of carboxyl groups, which are often introduced during the functionalization of carbon dots and are known to enhance their dispersibility and potential for surface interactions with other molecules. Furthermore, peaks observed at 1079 cm−1 and 1275 cm−1 were characteristic of CF stretching and CH bending frequencies, respectively [38]. The CF bonds, though less common in organic structures, suggest the introduction of fluorine atoms, possibly as impurities or through interactions with the synthesis environment. This can lead to altered surface chemistry and potentially impact the hydrophobicity or fluorescence behavior of the NFS-CDs. Overall, the FTIR results confirm the successful transfer of sulfur-containing groups from the L-cysteine precursor molecules onto the NFS-CD surface during the synthesis process. The combination of nitrogen and sulfur doping, along with the functional groups present, is expected to contribute to the unique optical, electronic, and chemical properties of the NFS-CDs.

The XRD pattern of NFS-CDs (Fig. 3b) demonstrates a broad diffraction peak at 2θ = 21.8°, associated with the (002) plane of a typical graphitic structure, which is characteristic of amorphous carbon [39]. This broadening of the diffraction peak resulted from the small size of the NFS-CDs, and further corroborated the HRTEM findings. The TEM images (Fig. 3c) show that the NFS-CDs are nearly spherical and monodisperse in water, without apparent aggregation. The HRTEM image (shown as an inset in Fig. 3c) displays a distinct lattice structure, indicating a crystal spacing of 0.21 nm, which corresponds to the (100) plane of graphitic carbon [40]. Statistical analysis of more than 100 particles indicated that the size distribution of the NFS-CDs ranged from 2 to 8 nm, with an average size of 5 nm (Fig. 3d). Moreover, even after several months of storage at room temperature, the dispersion of NFS-CDs in water maintained its transparency and homogeneity, indicating its exceptional stability. These characteristics highlight the potential of the NFS-CDs for various applications, particularly in fields requiring stable and well-dispersed nanoparticles.

The valence states and elemental compositions of the NFS-CDs were characterized using XPS. The full XPS spectrum (Fig. 4 a) displays four prominent peaks at binding energies of 400.9 eV, 687.3 eV, 163.3 eV, 286.4 eV, and 531.6 eV, corresponding to the elements N 1s, F 1s, S 2p, C 1s, and O 1s, respectively. The atomic ratios were calculated as N (7.38 %), F (4.47 %), S (8.64 %), C (69.13 %), and O (10.38 %). In the N 1s spectrum of the NFS-CDs (Fig. 4b), two peaks were identified: one at 400.1 eV corresponding to CNC bonds and another at 401.5 eV corresponding to NH bonds [41]. The F 1s spectrum (Fig. 4c) displays two peaks at 683.9 eV and 687.6 eV, attributed to semi-ionic CF and covalent CF groups, respectively [42]. The deconvoluted S 2p spectrum reveals two peaks at 166.2 eV and 164.9 eV, indicating the presence of CS (S 2p1/2 and S 2p3/2) units with no significant presence of oxidized sulfur groups. The C 1s spectrum exhibits four peaks at binding energies of 284.9 eV, 286.1 eV, 286.5 eV, and 287.3 eV, which can be ascribed to CC/CN, CO, CO, and CF bonds, respectively. The high-resolution O 1s spectra can be divided into three peaks around 530.1 eV, 530.8 eV, and 532.0 eV, representing CO, CO, and OCO bonds [43], respectively. These detailed XPS results confirmed the successful synthesis and precise doping of the NFS-CDs, with distinct contributions from the nitrogen, fluorine, and sulfur atoms incorporated into the carbon dot structure. This comprehensive elemental analysis underscores the effective integration of dopant atoms, which is crucial for enhanced optical and electronic properties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective and sensitive detection of Hg2+ ions
ce:section.ce:para: The selective and sensitive detection of Hg2+ using nitrogen, fluorine, and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (NFS-CDs) was investigated. The FL intensities of NFS-CDs were analyzed in the presence of various metal ions, including Ag+, Cd2+, Ce3+, Cr3+, Co2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Sn2+, and Zn2+ ions (10 × 10−6 M) in pH ∼ 7.4 PBS buffer solutions to evaluate their selectivity as a FL probe for Hg2+ detection. Fig. 5 a shows that the FL of the NFS-CDs was significantly enhanced at 490 nm and quenched at 580 nm in the presence of Hg2+ ions. In contrast, negligible FL changes were observed with other ions, indicating the excellent specificity of the NFS-CDs towards Hg2+ ions. This selectivity may be due to the chelating reaction of Hg2+ ions with the NFS-CDs, facilitated by the surface sulfur, carboxylic, and amine groups, enabling effective coordination with nitrogen and carbonyl oxygen atoms [30]. Further analysis (Fig. 5b) showed that the FL intensities of the Hg2+ ions were significantly lower than those of the other metal ions, confirming the superior selectivity of the NFS-CDs for Hg2+ ions. Additionally, Fig. 5c demonstrates that only Hg2+ ions led to substantial FL development of the NFS-CDs under both daylight and UV lamp irradiation at 365 nm, consistent with the FL spectra. These findings highlight the highly selective nature of the NFS-CDs as FL sensors for Hg2+ over other metal ions. The high selectivity of the NFS-CDs for Hg2+ ions can be ascribed to the interaction between Hg2+ ions and the functional groups present on the surface of the NFS-CDs. This interaction brought the NFS-CDs close together, facilitating an effective electron-transfer process that accelerated the non-radiative combination of excitons [44,45]. There was a substantial decrease in the FL of the NFS-CDs when exposed to Hg2+ ions. This phenomenon highlights the intricate interplay between the surface chemistry of the NFS-CDs and their responses to specific analytes, underscoring their potential as highly selective sensors for Hg2+. To further elucidate the identification performance of the NFS-CDs for Hg2+, titration experiments were conducted, and the results are shown in Fig. 6 a. Under excitation at 390 nm, as the Hg2+ ions concentration increased from 0 to 10 × 10−6 M in pH∼7.4 PBS buffer solutions, the FL emission intensity at 490 nm gradually rose, accompanied by the development of a blue-shifted emission peak at 580 nm. These two emission bands intersected at the isosbestic point at 550 nm, indicating the formation of the NFS-CDs/Hg2+ complexes [46].

The FL ratio at 490 and 580 nm (F490 nm/F580 nm) varied with the addition of Hg2+ ions, reaching saturation after the total addition of 10 × 10−6 M of Hg2+ ions (Fig. 6b). Furthermore, a mixture of NFS-CDs/Hg2+ complex exhibited enhanced FL emission at 490 nm with increasing concentrations (0–10 × 10−6 M), reaching the strongest FL emission intensity under both daylight and UV lamp irradiation at 365 nm (Fig. 6c), consistent with the results of FL spectra (Fig. 6a). The concentration of Hg2+ ions showed an excellent linear relationship with the FL intensity of NFS-CDs in the range of 0–10 × 10−6 M, as depicted in the Benesi-Hildebrand and Stern-Volmer plots (Fig. S1a,b). The association and Stern-Volmer constants were calculated to be 11 × 107 M−2 and 6 × 106 M−2, respectively, approaching the values derived from FL titration, indicating a strong affinity of NFS-CDs for Hg2+ ions [46]. The LOD of NFS-CDs for Hg2+ ions was calculated to be 18.0 and 67.5 × 10−9 M at 490 and 580 nm (Fig. S2), respectively, according to the equation (K × SD/S) [47], demonstrating a low detection limit.

The obtained limit of detection (LOD) for Hg2+ ions in this study is satisfactory when compared to previously reported values in the literature (as shown in Table S1). Although lower LOD have been reported for Hg2+ ions detection in some studies, the method developed here offers several distinct advantages. These include a broader linear detection range, ensuring reliable performance over a wider concentration of mercury ions. Additionally, the method exhibits excellent selectivity, enabling it to accurately detect Hg2+ ions even in the presence of other potentially interfering ions. To delve deeper into the selectivity of the NFS-CDs for Hg2+ ions compared with other metal ions, interference experiments were conducted as shown in Fig. 7 . Competitive analysis revealed that the presence of other analytes resulted in minimal-to-zero interference in the detection of Hg2+ (Fig. 7a and b). The ratio of the ratiometric spectral changes remained consistent despite the presence of other metal ions such as Ag+, Cd2+, Ce3+, Cr3+, Co2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Sn2+, and Zn2+ (100 × 10−6 M), highlighting the robust selectivity of NFS-CDs for Hg2+ complex. Additionally, the FL color changes of the NFS-CDs/Hg2+ complex in the presence of other ions were insignificant under both daylight and UV lamp irradiation at 365 nm (Fig. 7c). This observation is consistent with the FL spectra, where the other samples exhibited bright green FL, further confirming the ability of the NFS-CDs to distinguish Hg2+ ions from other chemically related analytes. These findings underscore the effectiveness of NFS-CDs as chemosensors for Hg2+ ions, demonstrating their ability to selectively detect Hg2+ ions even in the presence of potentially interfering metal ions. This high selectivity is crucial for the reliable and accurate detection of Hg2+ ions in various environmental and analytical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reversibility of the NFS-CDs towards Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: Reversibility is a critical criterion for practical applications of chemosensors. Therefore, the reversibility of the NFS-CDs/Hg2+ complex was investigated using EDTA as a chelating agent. Upon the addition of Hg2+ ions, a dramatic enhancement of the FL intensity of the NFS-CDs at 490 nm was observed in its FL spectrum. Subsequently, upon the addition of EDTA to the NFS-CDs/Hg2+ solution, the FL spectra were nearly restored to the original state of the NFS-CDs in the absence of Hg2+ [48]. This indicates successful regeneration of the NFS-CDs, as depicted in the corresponding solution contrast images in Fig. 8 a and 8b. The capacity for regeneration and reversibility plays a pivotal role in the advancement of novel chemical-sensing probes. These findings suggest that the NFS-CD chemosensor holds promise for real-time analysis because it can undergo reversible changes upon exposure to Hg2+ and subsequent removal. This reversibility enhances the practical utility and versatility of the NFS-CDs for various sensing applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of NFS-CDs for Hg2+ in real samples analysis
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the practical application of the NFS-CDs in sensing Hg2+ ions, real water samples collected from a local region of the campus were analyzed. Water samples were spiked with standard Hg2+ ions at predefined concentrations, followed by the NFS-CD analysis. Subsequently, the samples were diluted within the working linear range and analyzed using the proposed method under optimized conditions. Table 1 presents the data obtained from these analyses. The recoveries of the NFS-CDs for detecting Hg2+ ions ranged from 98.9 % to 104.6 %, indicating their excellent accuracy for detecting Hg2+ ions in natural water samples [45,48]. Additionally, all the relative standard deviations (RSD) values were within 2.47 %, suggesting high precision and consistency in the measurements. These results imply that the NFS-CDs can serve as precise and feasible chemosensors for the sensing of Hg2+ ions in real samples, demonstrating their potential for practical applications in environmental monitoring and water quality assessments.

The antibacterial activities of the NFS-CDs and their metal complexes were investigated through a series of experiments using E. coli and S. aureus as the test organisms. Both dark and light conditions were considered in the experiments. Control experiments without samples were conducted under identical conditions. As illustrated in Fig. 9 , the control plates did not exhibit any significant decrease in colony-forming units (CFU) [49]. However, treatment with NFS-CDs resulted in a notable reduction in CFU, indicating excellent inhibitory effects against both S. aureus (49.3 ± 2.8 %) and E. coli (55.1 ± 1.9 %). This inhibitory effect can be attributed to the presence of N, S, and F attached to the surface of the CDs, enhancing their antibacterial properties [50,51]. Remarkably, the NFS-CDs/Hg2+ complex exhibited significantly higher antibacterial activity against both test bacteria than the NFS-CDs alone. The survival rates of E. coli and S. aureus were 78.7 ± 2.6 % and 83.4 ± 2.3 %, respectively, indicating a substantial improvement in antimicrobial function when Hg2+ ions were introduced. Interestingly, the NFS-CDs/Hg2+ complex demonstrated even more potent antibacterial activity than the NFS-CDs alone [52]. Notably, the growth of E. coli was significantly inhibited by the NFS-CDs/Hg2+ complex, similar to the inhibitory effect of the NFS-CDs. These findings highlight the broad-spectrum antibacterial activity of the NFS-CDs and their metal complexes and their potential as effective antimicrobial agents for various applications.

Fluorescence imaging experiments were performed on live cells to assess the practical application of nitrogen-, fluorine-, and sulfur-codoped carbon dots (NFS-CDs) as a chemosensor for biological samples. These experiments utilized a fluorescence microscope to track the behavior of the NFS-CDs within living cells, specifically focusing on their interaction with mercury ions (Hg2+). Before fluorescence imaging, cytotoxicity tests were conducted using HCT116 cells (a human colon cancer cell line) to determine the safety and biocompatibility of NFS-CDs at various concentrations. The WST assay, which measures cell viability, was used to evaluate the impact of NFS-CDs at concentrations ranging from 0 to 150 μg/mL, as presented in Fig. 10 a. The results showed a concentration-dependent inhibition of cell growth at concentrations up to 100 μg/mL, cell viability remained high, exceeding 98 %, demonstrating minimal cytotoxicity [53]. This suggests that the NFS-CDs at this concentration are biocompatible and safe for use in live cell experiments. However, toxicity became apparent at concentrations exceeding 150 μg/mL, where a significant reduction in cell growth was observed [54]. Based on these results, the concentration of 100 μg/mL was chosen for subsequent imaging experiments. This concentration not only minimized toxicity but also maintained strong fluorescence properties, making it ideal for use in biological imaging applications.

To assess the efficacy of NFS-CDs as a probe for detecting Hg2+ ions in living cells, fluorescence imaging was performed on HCT116 cells incubated with 100 μg/mL of NFS-CDs. Fluorescence images were captured both before and after the introduction of Hg2+ ions into the cellular environment (Fig. 10b). The NFS-CDs displayed bright and distinct fluorescence signals within the cells, indicating their successful uptake and distribution across the intracellular space [55]. This highlights the good cell membrane permeability of the NFS-CDs, allowing them to enter cells and exhibit fluorescence without any additional modifications. Upon incubation with Hg2+, a significant increase in fluorescence intensity was observed within the cells. This enhanced fluorescence response is attributed to the selective interaction between Hg2+ ions and the surface functional groups of the NFS-CDs, which triggers a change in the fluorescence signal [56]. To confirm the localization of the NFS-CDs and their response to Hg2+, overlay images combining fluorescence and bright-field views were analyzed. These overlay images clearly demonstrated that the fluorescence signals were localized within the intracellular region, further supporting the notion that the NFS-CDs had effectively penetrated the cell membrane and distributed throughout the cytoplasm [57]. The enhanced fluorescence response following Hg2+ exposure also indicated the intracellular distribution of Hg2+ ions.

The results of these live cell imaging experiments demonstrated the capability of NFS-CDs to monitor the presence of Hg2+ ions inside living cells. The strong fluorescence signals, combined with the excellent cell viability at appropriate concentrations, underscore the potential of NFS-CDs as effective tools for intracellular detection and imaging of Hg2+ in biological systems. These findings highlight the promise of NFS-CDs for applications in fluorescence-based bioimaging [58,59], biological sensing, and potentially in medical diagnostics for detecting toxic heavy metals [60] such as mercury within cells. The limit of detection (LOD) for Hg2+ using NFS-CDs is 18.0 × 10−9 M at 490 nm and 67.5 × 10−9 M at 580 nm, indicating high sensitivity to mercury ions. In live-cell imaging experiments using HCT116 cells, NFS-CDs at a concentration of 100 μg/mL exhibited significant fluorescence after exposure to Hg2+, confirming their ability to detect intracellular mercury. This highlights the biological relevance of NFS-CDs for real-time monitoring of Hg2+, demonstrating their potential as highly sensitive tools for environmental and cellular mercury detection.

The potential use of NFS-CDs for both in vitro and in vivo applications significantly broadens the scope and relevance of their findings. In in vitro applications, NFS-CDs can serve as highly sensitive fluorescent probes for detecting Hg2+ in biological fluids, tissue samples, and cultured cells, offering real-time monitoring of toxic mercury exposure in various biomedical and environmental contexts. Their low cytotoxicity and biocompatibility, demonstrated by minimal cell growth inhibition at concentrations up to 100 μg/mL, make them suitable for live-cell imaging and other diagnostic assays. For in vivo applications, NFS-CDs excellent fluorescence properties, coupled with their selective binding to Hg2+, suggest their potential for tracking mercury accumulation in live organisms, enabling the detection of mercury toxicity in tissues or monitoring mercury metabolism. Furthermore, their reversibility when exposed to chelating agents like EDTA enhances their utility in dynamic, real-time sensing scenarios, both for therapeutic monitoring and environmental toxin exposure studies. These characteristics position NFS-CDs as promising tools for a wide range of biomedical and environmental applications.

In this study, nitrogen-, fluorine-, and sulfur-codoped carbon dots (NFS-CDs) were successfully synthesized and comprehensively characterized in terms of their optical, surface, morphological, and elemental properties. The NFS-CDs demonstrated remarkable selectivity and sensitivity towards Hg2+ ions, as evidenced by the significant FL intensity changes. The detection limits for Hg2+ were calculated to be 18.0 × 10−9 M at 490 nm and 67.5 × 10−9 M at 580 nm, indicating high sensitivity. This selectivity is attributed to the strong affinity between Hg2+ ions and the surface functional groups of the NFS-CDs, facilitating effective chelation and the subsequent FL response. In practical applications, the NFS-CDs exhibited excellent performance in detecting Hg2+ ions in natural water samples, with recovery rates ranging from 98.9 % to 104.6 % and RSD values within 2.47 %, demonstrating their feasibility and reliability in real-world scenarios. Moreover, the NFS-CDs in the presence of Hg2+ ions displayed considerable antibacterial activity, inhibiting the growth of E. coli and S. aureus by 78.7 ± 2.6 % and 83.4 ± 2.3 %, respectively. The presence of Hg2+ ions further enhanced this antibacterial effect, demonstrating the potential of the NFS-CDs in antimicrobial applications. Additionally, live cell imaging experiments showed that NFS-CDs are biocompatible and effective for intracellular Hg2+ detection, with high cell viability (>98 % at 100 μg/mL) and strong fluorescence signals, indicating good cell-membrane permeability and intracellular localization. Overall, the NFS-CDs synthesized in this study exhibited outstanding optical properties, structural stability, selective and sensitive detection capabilities for Hg2+ ions, and significant antibacterial activity. Their biocompatibility and effectiveness for intracellular imaging further enhance their potential for use in medical diagnostics and environmental monitoring. These multifunctional characteristics make NFS-CDs highly promising candidates for a range of applications, including biological sensing, environmental remediation, and medical diagnostics. The combination of their optical, sensing, and biological properties suggests that NFS-CDs could play a critical role in the development of future technologies in these fields.
